edges.description: Disease-causing variants destabilize the BBSome or its assembly machinery.
An incompletely assembled BBSome fails to move selected cargos correctly through the ciliary compartment.
Impaired neuronal ciliary trafficking perturbs hypothalamic satiety signaling.
High-throughput photoreceptor cargo delivery fails when ciliary trafficking is disrupted.
Kidney epithelial cilia-dependent homeostasis is disrupted downstream of trafficking failure.
Developmental ciliary signaling defects perturb limb-bud patterning.
Impaired satiety signaling increases hunger and food-seeking behavior.
Chronic positive energy balance drives early-onset obesity.
Photoreceptor degeneration produces the invariant retinal dystrophy phenotype.
Ongoing photoreceptor loss causes worsening visual acuity over time.
Structural and functional kidney disease is a major clinical consequence.
Disrupted limb-bud patterning manifests clinically as postaxial extra digits.

evidence.snippet: ARL6 | ARF like GTPase 6 | hgnc:13210 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: BBS loss perturbs cilia-dependent Sonic hedgehog signaling during limb-bud development, predisposing to postaxial digit duplication.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic interaction between Bardet-Biedl syndrome genes and implications for limb patterning.
evidence.snippet: We evaluated zebrafish fin bud patterning and observed altered Sonic hedgehog (shh) expression and subsequent changes to fin skeletal elements.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic characterization of Italian patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome and correlation to ocular, renal and audio-vestibular phenotype: identification of eleven novel pathogenic sequence variants.
evidence.snippet: The most common mutated gene was BBS1 followed by BBS10.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic and Genetic Features of Bardet Biedl Syndrome in a German Cohort.
evidence.snippet: The most common associated genes were BBS10 (32.8%) and BBS1 (24.6%), and by far the most commonly observed variants were BBS10 c.271dup;p.C91Lfs*5 (21 alleles) and BBS1 c.1169T>G;p.M390R (18 alleles).
BBS1 | Bardet-Biedl syndrome 1 | hgnc:966 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

meta.frequency: one of the most prevalent BBS genes

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic and Genetic Features of Bardet Biedl Syndrome in a German Cohort.
evidence.snippet: The most common associated genes were BBS10 (32.8%) and BBS1 (24.6%), and by far the most commonly observed variants were BBS10 c.271dup;p.C91Lfs*5 (21 alleles) and BBS1 c.1169T>G;p.M390R (18 alleles).

evidence.reference_title: Genetic characterization of Italian patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome and correlation to ocular, renal and audio-vestibular phenotype: identification of eleven novel pathogenic sequence variants.
evidence.snippet: BBS10 was associated with the worse outcome in terms of the renal, ocular and audiovestibular phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome have hyperleptinemia suggestive of leptin resistance.
evidence.snippet: BBS1 (27%) and BBS10 (30%) mutations were the most prevalent.
BBS10 | Bardet-Biedl syndrome 10 | hgnc:26291 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

meta.frequency: one of the most prevalent BBS genes

evidence.snippet: BBS2 | Bardet-Biedl syndrome 2 | hgnc:967 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: Failure of an intact BBSome disrupts trafficking of signaling proteins and other cargos into and within the ciliary compartment.

evidence.reference_title: Ciliary signaling proteins are mislocalized in the brains of Bardet-Biedl syndrome 1-null mice.
evidence.snippet: GPCR ciliary localization is disrupted in neurons from mouse models of the ciliopathy Bardet-Biedl syndrome, with GPCRs failing to localize to cilia, indicating the Bardet-Biedl syndrome proteins are required for trafficking of G protein-coupled receptors into neuronal cilia.

nodes.description: Pathogenic variants in core BBSome subunits and chaperonin-like BBS proteins impair ordered assembly of the BBSome, the ciliary trafficking complex central to BBS biology.

evidence.reference_title: Intrinsic protein-protein interaction-mediated and chaperonin-assisted sequential assembly of stable bardet-biedl syndrome protein complex, the BBSome.
evidence.snippet: Three additional BBS genes (BBS6, BBS10, and BBS12) have homology to type II chaperonins and interact with CCT/TRiC proteins and BBS7 to form a complex termed the BBS-chaperonin complex. This complex is required for BBSome assembly.

evidence.reference_title: BBS6, BBS10, and BBS12 form a complex with CCT/TRiC family chaperonins and mediate BBSome assembly.
evidence.snippet: Our data demonstrate that BBS6, BBS10, and BBS12 are necessary for BBSome assembly, and that impaired BBSome assembly contributes to the etiology of BBS phenotypes associated with the loss of function of these three BBS genes.

nodes.description: Pathologic hunger and food-seeking behavior contribute to the obesity burden in BBS.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperphagia among patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Total hyperphagia questionnaire score was higher in BBS than controls (27.6 ± 9.0 vs. 19.1 ± 7.9, P = 0.005).

nodes.description: BBS proteins are required for leptin-receptor trafficking and leptin-induced hypothalamic signaling, creating ciliary leptin resistance and impaired satiety signaling.

evidence.reference_title: Requirement of Bardet-Biedl syndrome proteins for leptin receptor signaling.
evidence.snippet: Our data indicate that BBS proteins mediate LepR trafficking and that impaired LepR signaling underlies energy imbalance in BBS.

evidence.reference_title: Patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome have hyperleptinemia suggestive of leptin resistance.
evidence.snippet: Patients with BBS had higher leptin than expected for their degree of adiposity, consistent with the notion that ciliopathy-induced leptin signaling dysfunction is associated with leptin resistance.
MKKS | MKKS centrosomal shuttling protein | hgnc:7108 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: Rapid early-childhood weight gain leads to persistent overweight or obesity through childhood and adulthood.

evidence.reference_title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: Weight patterns and genetics in a rare obesity syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Despite normal birth weight, most individuals with BBS experience rapid weight gain in early childhood, with high rates of overweight/obesity sustained through adolescence.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive clinical and genetic characterization of Bardet-Biedl Syndrome: insights from the largest Turkish cohort.
evidence.snippet: Retinal dystrophy was the most common feature (94.1%), followed by polydactyly (88.0%), obesity (68.0%), and renal anomalies (52.0%).
HP:0001513 | Obesity | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic variants in established BBS genes initiate classic Bardet-Biedl syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: Antenatal presentation of forty-five fetuses with biallelic pathogenic variants in known Bardet-Biedl syndrome genes.
evidence.snippet: Molecular diagnosis during pregnancies remains a timely challenge for this heterogeneous disease (22 known genes).

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive clinical and genetic characterization of Bardet-Biedl Syndrome: insights from the largest Turkish cohort.
evidence.snippet: Biallelic pathogenic variants were identified in all patients, including three novel variants in the ARL6/BBS3, BBS9, and BBS10 genes, observed in four patients, including two siblings.
A rare genetic multisystem disorder characterized by the variable association of retinal dystrophy, obesity, polydactyly, genitourinary and kidney anomalies, learning disability and hypogonadism

nodes.description: In photoreceptors, defective ciliary transport mislocalizes rhodopsin and other outer-segment cargo, leading to photoreceptor degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Bbs2-null mice have neurosensory deficits, a defect in social dominance, and retinopathy associated with mislocalization of rhodopsin.
evidence.snippet: Photoreceptor cell death is preceded by mislocalization of rhodopsin, indicating a defect in transport.

nodes.description: Postaxial extra digits affecting hands or feet are a cardinal developmental manifestation of BBS.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive clinical and genetic characterization of Bardet-Biedl Syndrome: insights from the largest Turkish cohort.
evidence.snippet: Retinal dystrophy was the most common feature (94.1%), followed by polydactyly (88.0%), obesity (68.0%), and renal anomalies (52.0%).

evidence.reference_title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: Antenatal presentation of forty-five fetuses with biallelic pathogenic variants in known Bardet-Biedl syndrome genes.
evidence.snippet: postaxial polydactyly (82%) and renal cysts (78%) were the most prevalent symptoms.
HP:0100259 | Postaxial polydactyly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Visual acuity progressively declines from childhood, often reaching severe impairment or blindness in later decades.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic and Genetic Features of Bardet Biedl Syndrome in a German Cohort.
evidence.snippet: Visual acuity decreased from ~0.2 (decimal) at age 5 to blindness 0 at 50 years.

evidence.reference_title: Visual acuity and retinal function in patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome.
evidence.snippet: In a relatively young cohort of patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome, only 21% had 20/40 or better vision.

nodes.description: Structural and functional kidney disease includes renal dysmorphism, cystic or dysplastic change, and impaired kidney function.

evidence.reference_title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: a study of the renal and cardiovascular phenotypes in a French cohort.
evidence.snippet: Renal abnormalities, including impairment of renal function and signs of chronic interstitial nephropathy of dysplastic nature, were documented in 82% of the patients.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive clinical and genetic characterization of Bardet-Biedl Syndrome: insights from the largest Turkish cohort.
evidence.snippet: Retinal dystrophy was the most common feature (94.1%), followed by polydactyly (88.0%), obesity (68.0%), and renal anomalies (52.0%).
HP:0012622 | Chronic kidney disease | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Kidney involvement in BBS includes renal dysmorphism, impaired kidney function, and chronic tubulointerstitial disease consistent with a renal ciliopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: a study of the renal and cardiovascular phenotypes in a French cohort.
evidence.snippet: Renal abnormalities, including impairment of renal function and signs of chronic interstitial nephropathy of dysplastic nature, were documented in 82% of the patients.

nodes.description: Invariant retinal dystrophy with early rod dysfunction, progressive retinal degeneration, and severe visual morbidity over time.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic and Genetic Features of Bardet Biedl Syndrome in a German Cohort.
evidence.snippet: All patients had retinal dystrophy with morphologic changes of the retina.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive clinical and genetic characterization of Bardet-Biedl Syndrome: insights from the largest Turkish cohort.
evidence.snippet: Retinal dystrophy was the most common feature (94.1%), followed by polydactyly (88.0%), obesity (68.0%), and renal anomalies (52.0%).
HP:0000556 | Retinal dystrophy | Obligate (100%)

nodes.description: MC4R agonist pharmacotherapy that bypasses upstream ciliopathy-related satiety-pathway dysfunction and reduces bodyweight in Bardet-Biedl syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of setmelanotide, a melanocortin-4 receptor agonist, in patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome and Alström syndrome: a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial with an open-label period.
evidence.snippet: 32·3% (95% CI 16·7 to 51·4; p=0·0006) of patients aged 12 years or older with Bardet-Biedl syndrome reached at least a 10% reduction in bodyweight after 52 weeks of setmelanotide.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of setmelanotide, a melanocortin-4 receptor agonist, in patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome and Alström syndrome: a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial with an open-label period.
evidence.snippet: These results support the use of setmelanotide and provided the necessary evidence for approval of this drug as the first treatment for obesity in patients with Bardet-Biedl syndrome.
